metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete jq proving command to AC1 that (a) extracts all IAM statements containing action `execute-api:Invoke` from the `terraform show -json tf.plan` output and (b) asserts their count is exactly 1, e.g.: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.. | objects | select(.actions? and (.actions[] | contains("execute-api:Invoke"))) ] | length == 1'` (exact path to be verified against the live plan JSON structure).
findings.finding: AC1's proving command for IAM exclusivity ('ONLY statement with action execute-api:Invoke is the one attached to rebar-opcert-admin') is described only in prose. No concrete jq expression is given to (a) extract all IAM statements with that action from the tf.plan JSON and (b) assert their count is exactly 1. A test that checks existence of the correct statement but omits the cardinality check would pass even if a second Invoke-granting statement were introduced, making the security invariant unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 — structured assertion block
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: 'The cardinality assertion uses `jq -e` on the structured plan JSON: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e \"[.. | objects | select(.actions? and (.actions[] | contains(\\"execute-api:Invoke\\"))) ] | length == 1\"` — this MUST return exit 0; any additional Invoke statement causes exit 1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` as a file to modify in the What/Scope sections (and assert in AC3 or a new AC) that it calls `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` before `docker compose up`, following the same pattern as the `materialize-g2p-config.sh` call at line 177.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` follows the 'existing secret-materialization precedent' and will call the new `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`, but compose-up.sh is never mentioned as a file to modify. Inspection shows compose-up.sh EXPLICITLY calls each materialize script (line 177 calls `materialize-g2p-config.sh`); the new script will not be invoked unless compose-up.sh is also updated. This is an unenumerated consumer: the plan scopes 'the new script is `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`' but omits the call-site.
findings.location: What section: 'On the box the guard value is materialized from its SSM parameter…by a materialize script following the stack's existing secret-materialization precedent (infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh — SSM → file, same pattern; the new script is infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet in the Compose section of What: 'Extend `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` to call `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` before `docker compose up` (same pattern as the `materialize-g2p-config.sh` call at line 177).' And add a test assertion in AC3 that compose-up.sh invokes the new script.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` as an edit target in the plan's 'What' / Scope sections, specifying exactly where the call to `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` is inserted (mirroring the `materialize-g2p-config.sh` invocation pattern at line 177).
findings.finding: infra/scripts/compose-up.sh is an unenumerated consumer that must be modified to call the new materialize-opcert-guard.sh script. Every existing materialize script (materialize-deploy-key.sh, materialize-g2p-config.sh) is explicitly called from compose-up.sh before `docker compose up` (lines 127 and 177). The new script follows the same pattern, but compose-up.sh is not listed as an edit target anywhere in the plan's 'What' section or Scope.
findings.location: What / Compose section; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh lines 127, 177
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Compose section: 'infra/scripts/compose-up.sh gains a call to infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh immediately before `docker compose up` (mirroring the materialize-g2p-config.sh invocation at line 177), with the same NON-FATAL wrapper pattern.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the API Gateway → nginx integration URI scheme and host explicitly (e.g., `https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/` with the domain name so TLS SNI resolves, or `http://` to a port that does NOT redirect), and confirm this is verified by the `terraform plan` + live round-trip AC.
findings.finding: The API Gateway → EC2 nginx backend integration URI scheme (http:// vs https://) is undeclared. Without a VPC Link, API Gateway must reach the box over its public Elastic IP. The existing nginx configuration on :80 returns a 301 redirect for all non-ACME traffic (API Gateway HTTP integrations do not follow redirects), and :443 requires TLS with a matching certificate (rebar.solutions.navateam.com). The plan says 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' without specifying the integration URL, leaving the implementer to guess a detail that determines whether the integration works at all.
findings.location: What section: 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the /opcert/ routes'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/` as the integration URI so TLS works via SNI with the existing Let's Encrypt cert. Add an nginx rule that accepts the guard header check on :443 before proxying to the opcert container port.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` must issue `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx` after writing the conf include, and that `compose-up.sh` must be updated to invoke this script (matching how it calls the existing materialize-*.sh scripts) before `docker compose up`.
findings.finding: The `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` script writes the origin-guard value into the nginx conf include, but the plan specifies no nginx reload step. The existing `materialize-deploy-key.sh` and `materialize-g2p-config.sh` precedents only write files to bind-mounted container directories and never touch nginx. A host nginx reload (`nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx`) is required for the guard value to take effect on a running box; without it, the include file is written but the old (or missing) guard value remains active.
findings.location: What section: 'a materialize script following the stack's existing secret-materialization precedent'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` to the scope of changes and specify that it must call `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (with a nginx reload) before `docker compose up`, following the established pattern for materialize-deploy-key.sh and materialize-g2p-config.sh.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` is the boot orchestrator that calls all existing materialize scripts (`materialize-deploy-key.sh`, `materialize-g2p-config.sh`) before `docker compose up`, but the plan does not specify that `compose-up.sh` must be updated to call the new `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`. Without this, the origin-guard value is never materialized into the nginx conf or service env on boot.
findings.location: What section: compose/nginx wiring; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh

findings.finding: AC1 asserts 'no other principal is granted [execute-api:Invoke]' but provides no proving command or test approach for this negative assertion. Asserting an absence across all IAM policy documents in the Terraform configuration (inline policies, resource-based policies, any future additions) requires exhaustive scanning, not just verifying the one IaC-created role. With no described approach, an executor has no guidance on how to implement this check, and a test that only verifies the `rebar-opcert-admin` role's inline policy exists would silently pass even if a second policy inadvertently also granted Invoke.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'A conftest (or equivalent) policy test scans all `aws_iam_role_policy`, `aws_iam_policy_document`, and `aws_iam_role` resources in `infra/terraform/` and asserts that `execute-api:Invoke` appears only in the `rebar-opcert-admin` inline policy; the test fails if any other resource document contains this action.'

findings.finding: infra/scripts/compose-up.sh is an unenumerated consumer that must be modified to call the new materialize-opcert-guard.sh script. Every existing materialize script (materialize-deploy-key.sh is called from infra/gerrit/setup-replication.sh; materialize-g2p-config.sh is called from compose-up.sh line 177) is wired into the boot/deploy orchestration flow. The new script at infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh will need a call site in compose-up.sh (before `docker compose up`) to materialize the guard value into the nginx conf at boot. The plan never names compose-up.sh as a file to be modified.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh (line 177 area)
findings.suggested_fix: In the What/Compose section add: 'infra/scripts/compose-up.sh gains a pre-up call to materialize-opcert-guard.sh (following the materialize-g2p-config.sh call at line 177), materialized before docker compose up.'

findings.finding: The API Gateway → EC2 nginx integration URI scheme (http:// vs https://) is undeclared, but the two options have materially different behaviors given the network topology (no VPC Link means traffic crosses the public internet to the box's EIP). The existing nginx :80 block (confirmed in infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template) issues a 301 redirect for all non-ACME traffic; API Gateway HTTP_PROXY integration returns that 301 to the caller rather than following it, silently breaking all proxied requests. Using https://:443 works but requires API Gateway to validate the origin's Let's Encrypt cert (fine) — yet this is an implementation choice with a wrong-default trap the plan leaves unresolved.
findings.location: What section — 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the /opcert/ routes'
findings.suggested_fix: Use the origin-guard-protected loopback path directly: since API Gateway HTTP_PROXY cannot reach 127.0.0.1 on the remote box, the integration URI should target `https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/` (the box's public HTTPS endpoint with its valid LE cert), or alternatively have nginx proxy only on :443 for the /opcert/ location. State this explicitly in the What section.

findings.finding: The new materialize script (`infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`) is never wired into a boot orchestrator, and the plan does not specify a `systemctl reload nginx` step. The entire existing precedent — `materialize-deploy-key.sh` and `materialize-g2p-config.sh` — writes to container bind-mount dirs and is explicitly called from `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` before `docker compose up`. nginx is a HOST service (not in compose, confirmed in docker-compose.yml and compose-up.sh). Writing the guard value to an nginx conf include has no effect until nginx reloads. Without both (a) a call-site in compose-up.sh and (b) a `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx` in the script or the orchestrator, the origin-guard check is never enforced — a direct-to-origin request without the guard header would NOT be rejected (AC5 third check).
findings.location: What section — compose/nginx wiring; materialize script; AC3 and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to compose-up.sh, immediately after the g2p config materialization block: `bash "${REPO_ROOT}/infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh"` — and have that script end with `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx` (fail-closed, matching the existing scripts' set -euo pipefail discipline). Document this in the What section.

findings.finding: `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` is an unenumerated consumer: every existing materialize script (`materialize-deploy-key.sh`, `materialize-g2p-config.sh`) is explicitly called from `compose-up.sh` before `docker compose up` (lines 177, 127). The new `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` follows that precedent, but the plan never enumerates `compose-up.sh` as a file requiring a call-site addition — leaving the guard value unmaterialized on every fresh boot.
findings.location: What section — Compose subsection; `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` lines 172-183
findings.suggested_fix: In the Compose subsection, add: 'extend `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` to call `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` before `docker compose up -d --build`, following the `materialize-g2p-config.sh` precedent (non-fatal: a missing guard must not block the whole stack from booting, but must emit a loud warning).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the API Gateway integration backend URI scheme and port explicitly (e.g., `https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/` or `http://127.0.0.1:<opcert-port>/` via a local loopback path), and confirm API Gateway HTTP_PROXY integration does not receive a 301 redirect from the chosen target.
findings.finding: The API Gateway → EC2 nginx integration URI scheme is unspecified, but the choice (http:// vs https://) is load-bearing: the existing nginx :80 server block issues a 301 redirect for all non-ACME traffic (confirmed in `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` lines 22-36), and AWS API Gateway HTTP_PROXY integrations do NOT follow 3xx redirects. If an implementer chooses `http://` (the natural reading of 'HTTP-integrated'), every proxied request receives a 301 and silently fails. The feasible path is https:// on port 443, but the plan never states this.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway HTTP API description: 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin'
findings.suggested_fix: If API Gateway reaches the box over its public Elastic IP, use `https://<domain>/opcert/` as the integration URI (the nginx :443 block proxies correctly). Alternatively, note that HTTP_PROXY without VPC Link hits the public interface — document the explicit URI scheme in the plan to avoid the http://→301 trap.

findings.finding: The plan says the API Gateway is 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the `/opcert/` routes' but never declares the integration URI scheme or port. The existing nginx config (`infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`) shows that port 80 issues a 301 redirect to HTTPS for all non-ACME traffic, so an `http://` integration URI pointing at port 80 would receive a redirect response rather than proxying to the opcert service. The integration must target `https://` on port 443 (or a loopback port where the opcert container is published), but the plan leaves this undeclared, making the integration URI unverified.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway paragraph: 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the `/opcert/` routes'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC4 (and the 'Deploy + prove' section) with the parent's superseding 'Authoritative era anchor' schema: replace `added_at_commit` with `added_at_log_position` (a tickets-branch log position stamped by `rebar trusted-env`), consistent with the parent epic's final resolution.
findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 uses `added_at_commit` as the era field name when pinning the deployed environment's public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, but the parent epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' section explicitly supersedes the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' schema: era boundaries are now `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions, operator-stamped by a `rebar trusted-env` helper), NOT `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` (main git SHAs). The leaf directly contradicts the parent's settled, superseding schema.
findings.location: AC4 / Leaf 'Deploy + prove' section; parent epic 6d0d 'Authoritative era anchor' section
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, change 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' to 'with `added_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log position, stamped by `rebar trusted-env`) per the parent epic's "Authoritative era anchor" resolution'. Update the 'Deploy + prove' bullet similarly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC4 test assertion to verify the public key field is present and is a valid Ed25519 public key string (e.g. matches `ssh-ed25519 AAAA...` format), not just that `env_id` matches.
findings.finding: AC4's test assertion only verifies that `env_id` matches `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`, but the described deliverable explicitly includes pinning the public key — 'the deployed environment's public key and `env_id` are pinned'. The test does not assert that the public key field is present and non-empty, leaving the key-pinning half of the deliverable untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'a test asserts the entry parses, the `env_id` matches `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`, and the `public_key` field is present and matches the Ed25519 public key format (e.g. `^ssh-ed25519 AAAA`).'

findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 pins the deployed environment's public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol', but the parent epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' section (security finding #5, Option B — era-at-storage-anchor) explicitly supersedes the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' and replaces `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` (explicit git SHAs on `main`) with **tickets-branch log positions** (`added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position`). The leaf cites the superseded schema, contradicting the parent's settled design.
findings.location: AC4 — 'The deployed environment's public key and `env_id` are pinned in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' in AC4 with 'with `added_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log position, stamped by the `rebar trusted-env` helper) per the epic's Option B era-at-storage-anchor protocol'. Verify the produced YAML entry matches the schema the epic's 'Authoritative era anchor' section defines before closing this story.

findings.finding: The leaf's AC4 specifies pinning the deployed public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol', but the parent's later 'Authoritative era anchor (security finding #5)' section explicitly SUPERSEDES the earlier 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' and renames the field to `added_at_log_position` (a tickets-branch log position, not a main-branch SHA). The leaf uses the old, superseded field name, contradicting the parent's current authoritative schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4 / `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` pinning
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, replace 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' with 'with `added_at_log_position` (the current tickets-branch tip, operator-stamped via the `rebar trusted-env` helper) per the epic's era protocol (finding #5 in the epic's "Authoritative era anchor" section)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the ticket's `file_impact` to replace `infra/opcert/` (non-existent, rejected design) with the actual edit targets: `infra/terraform/` (Terraform extension) and `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (new script), and `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` (nginx route addition).
findings.finding: The ticket's `file_impact` metadata lists `infra/opcert/` as the Terraform edit target, but the plan body specifies that all Terraform work goes into the existing `infra/terraform/` module. The path `infra/opcert/` does not exist in the repository and is the rejected design path (the dedicated module approach that was ruled out for cost reasons). This discrepancy means the file_impact field was never updated after the design decision moved.
findings.location: ticket file_impact metadata; What / Terraform section

findings.finding: AC1 mandates structured JSON-plan parsing ONLY for the IAM policy assertion; the remaining declaration assertions — `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on routes, `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` on the key parameter, `hashicorp/random` in `required_providers`, the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping, and the two SecureString parameter declarations — are described only as 'A test asserts these declarations exist,' leaving them implementable as HCL source-greps. A grep of `.tf` source files would pass even if Terraform generated a broken or misconfigured plan, constituting the change-detector/source-grep anti-pattern.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, paragraph beginning 'infra/terraform/ declares'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, replace 'A test asserts these declarations exist' with: 'A test parses `terraform plan -json` output and asserts each of the following appears in the planned resource graph: (a) both API Gateway routes have `authorization_type = AWS_IAM`; (b) both SSM parameter resources are of type `aws_ssm_parameter` with `type = SecureString`; (c) the key parameter resource has `lifecycle.ignore_changes` containing `value`; (d) the integration resource includes a static `requestParameters` entry for `X-Opcert-Guard`; (e) `required_providers` in the provider lock includes `hashicorp/random`.' This mirrors the existing IAM-policy assertion pattern already mandated in AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify the backend integration URI scheme: assert that the APIGW→nginx origin integration uses `https://` (the box's TLS-terminated port 443) and add this to the Terraform spec and AC1, closing the cleartext-guard-exposure risk.
findings.finding: The API Gateway → EC2 origin integration protocol is unspecified. The plan states the API Gateway is 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' and 'over TLS' — but 'over TLS' qualifies the client-facing invocation path, not the backend leg. Since no VPC Link is used, the APIGW→EC2 backend hop traverses the public internet. If the backend integration URI is `http://` (plain HTTP), the `X-Opcert-Guard` header value travels in cleartext, a passive observer can extract it and bypass the guard by hitting nginx port 80 directly (which the security group already opens for ACME). No AC or plan text specifies the backend integration URI scheme (http:// vs https://) or asserts TLS on that leg.
findings.location: What section — 'an API Gateway HTTP API (v2) … over TLS, HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin for the `/opcert/` routes, injecting a shared origin-guard header'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform spec and AC1, change 'HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx origin' to 'HTTPS-integrated to the box's nginx origin at https://<domain>/opcert/ (port 443, TLS, same cert served by Let's Encrypt)' — this ensures the X-Opcert-Guard value is encrypted in transit on the APIGW→EC2 leg and the guard's confidentiality is actually enforced.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `prevent_destroy = true` to the `lifecycle` block of the `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` SSM parameter declaration (matching the pattern already used for `aws_ebs_volume.data` and `aws_eip.gerrit`), and add an AC assertion that the plan JSON shows this safeguard.
findings.finding: The plan places the Ed25519 private key exclusively in SSM Parameter `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` — but no `prevent_destroy` is specified on that parameter. A `terraform destroy` or `terraform destroy -target aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_ed25519_key` would irrecoverably delete the only copy of the private key, since the plan explicitly states 'the key exists nowhere else'.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store declarations; AC1 (`lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `hashicorp/random` provider + `random_password` resource is necessary, or whether the origin-guard can be operator-generated (e.g. `openssl rand -hex 32`) and seeded into SSM like every other secret, with Terraform using a `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` read to inject the value into the API Gateway static header — eliminating the new provider dependency while preserving the closed-loop guarantee.
findings.finding: The `hashicorp/random` provider is introduced with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three violated), solely for `random_password` to generate the origin-guard value. Every other one-time secret in the codebase (all 12 params in `rebar_secret_params`) follows the established pattern: generate out-of-band, seed SSM, use `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`. A Terraform `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` reading an operator-seeded guard value would close the same API-Gateway-header ↔ SSM loop without a new provider dependency.
findings.location: What section — Terraform bullets: 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `random_password` with: (1) add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` to the existing `rebar_secret_params` list in `ssm.tf` (placeholder + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`), (2) add a `data "aws_ssm_parameter" "opcert_guard"` read (with `with_decryption = true`), and (3) use `data.aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_guard.value` as the API Gateway static header value — matching the out-of-band-seed pattern used for all 12 other secrets. Remove `hashicorp/random` from `required_providers`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the random_password replacement / origin-guard desync risk: either add a lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] } on the random_password resource (preventing Terraform from ever regenerating it), document a mandatory re-seeding procedure, or replace random_password with the codebase-established operator-seed placeholder pattern (matching ssm.tf's existing 12 secrets) so the guard value is stable and managed out-of-band.
findings.finding: If the `random_password` resource generating the origin-guard value is ever replaced (state taint, state loss, provider upgrade, or explicit `terraform taint`), Terraform will silently update the API Gateway static `X-Opcert-Guard` header to the new value while the SSM parameter `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` retains the old value (guarded by `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`), permanently desynchronizing the two halves of the origin-guard loop. The plan specifies no re-seeding procedure for this scenario.
findings.location: What section: origin-guard provisioning path / random_password / lifecycle
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `random_password` with the established operator-seed pattern: declare `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` as a placeholder SecureString (like all 12 existing secrets in ssm.tf), use a `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` source to read the operator-seeded value, and map it to the API Gateway static header. This eliminates the new `hashicorp/random` provider dependency, aligns with existing codebase conventions, and makes the guard value stable across Terraform state operations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify or eliminate the `hashicorp/random` provider: either explain why Terraform-managed generation is preferred over the codebase's established operator-seed pattern (CHANGEME + lifecycle ignore_changes) for the origin-guard value, or replace it with the existing pattern (operator generates a random value, seeds it via `aws ssm put-parameter`, Terraform declares the slot with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`).
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) as a new Terraform provider with exactly one use-site (the origin-guard value), while the codebase has an established, uniform pattern for all SSM SecureString parameters: `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` + operator CLI seeding (`ssm.tf`, 12 entries). No justification is given for the novel approach over the existing prior art. Beyond the anti-repackaging concern, the new provider introduces a silent-rotation risk: if the `random_password` resource is replaced (state taint, state loss, provider upgrade), Terraform updates the API Gateway static header to the new value while the nginx materialize script still reads the old SSM SecureString value — breaking the origin-guard check with no error signal.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`… the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the existing ssm.tf pattern: add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` to `rebar_secret_params` (or declare it separately with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` and `value = "CHANGEME"`), document the operator step `aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard --value "$(openssl rand -hex 32)" --type SecureString --overwrite`, and read it in Terraform via `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` to inject into the API Gateway integration — no new provider required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit recovery procedure to the deploy section: specify that the generated private key MUST be backed up (e.g. to a second SSM parameter with restricted access, or an operator-controlled secret store) before the local copy is discarded, and describe the re-keying steps (regenerate, re-seed, re-pin in trusted_environments.yaml, commit) in case the parameter is lost.
findings.finding: The private key seeding step is explicitly described as irreversible ('the key exists nowhere else') — a destructive, unrecoverable commitment — but the plan provides no explicit back-out procedure or recovery path (e.g. re-keying, re-pinning) if the SSM parameter is accidentally deleted, the parameter value is corrupted, or the operator's local copy is lost before seeding completes.
findings.location: What section — deploy procedure; 'seed the SecureString (the key exists nowhere else)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify why `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` departs from the `infra/gerrit/` precedent directory, or relocate it to `infra/gerrit/` to match the established pattern (and update all path references accordingly).
findings.finding: The plan makes a confident codebase assertion that the new materialize script follows 'the infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh — SSM → file, same pattern' precedent, yet places the new script in `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (a different directory). The two precedent scripts live in `infra/gerrit/` (confirmed). This directory-level inconsistency means the 'same pattern' claim is factually misleading about where the pattern lives, and the compose-up.sh call-site paths will also differ (it references `${REPO_ROOT}/infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh`).
findings.location: What section: 'following the stack's existing secret-materialization precedent (infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh — SSM → file, same pattern; the new script is infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the claim that 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' (HTTP API v2 does support resource policies); document whether a resource policy (e.g. deny-all-except-account) is intentionally omitted or should be added as defense-in-depth.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the sole rationale for restricting access purely via identity policy on the IaC-created admin role. This claim is factually incorrect — API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies. The false premise could cause the implementer to skip a resource policy that would provide defense-in-depth (deny all unless source-account matches), and it suggests the plan has not evaluated whether the identity-policy-only approach is the best available restriction mechanism.
findings.location: What section: 'an HTTP API has no resource policy'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct the claim 'an HTTP API has no resource policy': AWS HTTP APIs do support resource-based policies (since 2022 GA); either add a resource policy as a defense-in-depth layer, or add an explicit ADR-style note explaining why it was considered and rejected.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the factual basis for using only an identity/inline policy on the IaC-created caller role, foreclosing a defense-in-depth option. This is a verifiable capability assertion that is factually incorrect: AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs have supported resource-based policies since their 2022 GA update, documented under 'Controlling access to HTTP APIs with AWS IAM'. The plan should either verify and correct this claim, or explicitly acknowledge that a resource policy was considered and rejected (with rationale).
findings.location: What section — 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with a verified statement, e.g.: 'HTTP APIs support resource-based policies but we rely solely on the identity-policy-based model (inline policy on `rebar-opcert-admin`) because [reason]; a resource policy was considered and rejected because [reason].' If the defense-in-depth option is desired, add `aws_apigatewayv2_api.body` or a resource policy attachment restricting `execute-api:Invoke` to the specific role ARN as a second enforcement point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a secure key-material disposal step to the seeding procedure: either (a) generate the key in a ramdisk / tmpfs, seed SSM, then shred and unmount; or (b) use `ssh-keygen -f /dev/stdout` piped directly to `aws ssm put-parameter --value "$(...)"` so no plaintext file touches disk, then document this exact command in the deploy procedure.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 private-key seeding procedure (`ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N ""` then `aws ssm put-parameter`) writes the private key to the operator's local filesystem by default, and the plan specifies no step to securely dispose of that local file after seeding. The plan's claim that the key 'exists nowhere else' is therefore not procedurally enforced, leaving a copy of the trust-anchor signing key on the operator's workstation.
findings.location: ## What — Deploy + prove section: 'generate the key locally (ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N "") and seed the SecureString'
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the two-step procedure with a single pipeline that never writes to disk:
```bash
aws ssm put-parameter \
  --name '/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key' \
  --type SecureString \
  --value "$(ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N '' -f /dev/stdin <<< '' 2>/dev/null | cat)" \
  --overwrite
```
Or generate into a tmpfs mount, seed SSM, then `shred -u` the file. Document the chosen approach in the deploy procedure so it is operator-repeatable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact nginx directive pattern for X-Opcert-Guard header enforcement in the What/compose section (e.g., 'use a `map` directive mapping the header value to a guard-match flag, combined with a `return 403` before `proxy_pass` in the location block') so the executor cannot accidentally choose a bypass-prone mechanism.
findings.finding: The nginx directive pattern for rejecting requests with a non-matching (or absent) X-Opcert-Guard header is unspecified. nginx has multiple mechanisms (the `if` directive in a location block, `auth_request`, a Lua/njs script, or a `map` + `return 403`) with materially different security semantics — the `if` directive in particular has well-known pitfalls in nginx location context. Leaving the choice to the executor creates a defaultable gap that could produce a bypass.
findings.location: What section — compose/nginx wiring paragraph; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the nginx wiring paragraph: 'The guard check MUST use a `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; "$guard_value" 1; }` block at the http level, with `if ($opcert_guard_ok = 0) { return 403; }` placed BEFORE `proxy_pass` in the `/opcert/` location — avoiding the nginx `if`-in-location proxy-pass bypass pitfall.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a new ADR (or a CLAUDE.md / `infra/docs/` design note) capturing: the two-layer security model (SigV4 outer + origin-guard inner), why no VPC Link is needed, the key-seeding procedure, and the guard-value rotation procedure — and reference it from the plan's 'Why' section.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel two-layer security architecture (API Gateway SigV4 + nginx origin-guard header) and a new operational key-seeding procedure, but does not create or update any ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or runbook to capture these as durable architectural decisions. Existing ADRs (0046, 0047) cover cost/isolation and compute placement — not the security model of the guard header pattern, its rotation procedure, or the rationale for the two-layer defense-in-depth approach.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria — no ADR creation step mentioned anywhere
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0048-opcert-gateway-security-model.md` (or the project's ADR numbering convention) covering: (1) decision to use API GW HTTP API SigV4 + nginx guard header as defense-in-depth without VPC Link, (2) key seeding steps and non-extractability guarantee, (3) guard-value rotation runbook (re-run `terraform apply` to regenerate, re-materialize on box). Reference from the plan and from `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` header comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling contract for `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`: non-zero exit on SSM error (propagated to compose healthcheck/startup), atomic write (tmp-file + rename), and log/alert on failure — and add an AC assertion that the script exits non-zero when the stub `aws` returns an error.
findings.finding: The materialize script (`infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`) has no specified error handling: if SSM is unavailable or returns an error, the script's behavior (silent empty file, non-zero exit, partial write) is unspecified. An empty or stale guard value would either cause nginx to silently pass all requests (if empty string matches) or reject all requests, with no alert surfaced.
findings.location: What section — 'Compose — extend infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'; AC bullet 3 (materialize script hermetic test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `set -euo pipefail` and write to a tmpfile then `mv` atomically; have compose declare a `healthcheck` that re-runs the script and a `restart: unless-stopped` policy so a materialize failure surfaces as a container restart rather than a silent bad state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability specification for the new failure points: at minimum a CloudWatch alarm on API Gateway 5xx rate, a structured log line (with count metric) for nginx guard-header rejections, and a compose `healthcheck` + `restart: unless-stopped` for the opcert service.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for the new failure points: the API Gateway endpoint, the nginx origin-guard rejection path, and the opcert compose service have no specified metrics, logs, or alerts. Operators cannot detect elevated 4xx/5xx rates, guard-header rejections, or service crashes without live testing.
findings.location: What section; AC bullet 5 (operator-attested live checks)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure covering: (a) stopping the compose service and removing the SSM parameters (or marking the key parameter as revoked), (b) reverting/removing the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entry via a revert commit, and (c) `terraform destroy` or targeted resource removal — and note which of these must happen before others to avoid a window where the gate is down but the trust pin is still active.
findings.finding: No rollback procedure is specified. Rolling back requires: (1) `terraform destroy` or targeted resource removal for the API Gateway + SSM parameters, (2) stopping/removing the compose service, and (3) reverting the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` commit. Steps 2 and 3 are unspecified, and step 3 requires a follow-on commit that could leave the trusted env pinned (and trusted by consumers) even after the service is gone.
findings.location: Scope section; AC bullet 4 (trusted_environments.yaml pin); AC bullet 5 (operator-attested deploy)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the key rotation and retirement lifecycle: specify (a) the ordered steps to atomically rotate the key (update SSM, update trusted_environments.yaml in a single commit, redeploy service), (b) ownership of who performs rotation and how the `lifecycle { ignore_changes }` guard is handled during intentional rotation, and (c) the retirement/cleanup path (remove SSM parameter, remove yaml entry, terraform destroy targeted resources).
findings.finding: The RETIRE/rotation lifecycle for the Ed25519 key (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`) and the corresponding public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` is unspecified. These two pieces of shared state must stay in sync: updating SSM without updating `trusted_environments.yaml` (or vice versa) breaks all trust verification. No ownership, procedure, or atomicity guarantee for rotation is defined.
findings.location: What section — SSM parameter declaration; AC bullet 4 (trusted_environments.yaml); Scope (In)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the schema (or reference the epic document that defines it) for `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` and explicitly include first-creation of this file as a deliverable in the What or AC4 section, since the file does not exist in the repo today.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` exists (or that its schema is pre-established by the epic's era protocol) and instructs pinning the deployed environment's public key + env_id into it. A repo-wide search finds no such file — `.rebar/` contains only a `cache/` subdirectory. This is first-creation work, but the plan treats it as an append-to operation and provides no schema or creation step for the file itself.
findings.location: What section / AC4: 'pinned in .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml (with added_at_commit per the epic's era protocol)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Create `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (if absent) with schema: `environments: [{env_id: <str>, public_key: <openssh-pubkey>, added_at_commit: <sha>}]`' and update AC4 to reference that schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` explicitly as an edit target in the 'What' section (or clarify whether the /opcert/ route goes into the existing template or a new nginx include snippet loaded from the materialize script), and add it to Scope / In.
findings.finding: infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template is an unenumerated edit target. The plan's AC3 says 'nginx config gain the opcert service and the guarded /opcert/ route', and the Compose section says 'an nginx route /opcert/ → the service that rejects requests whose X-Opcert-Guard header does not match' — but infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template (the sole nginx config file in the repo) is never named as a file being modified in the 'What' section or Scope / In list.
findings.location: What / Compose section; AC3; infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan whether the `opcert` compose service uses any `external: true` volumes; if yes, enumerate extending `SITE_SUBDIRS` in `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` and confirm config-check.sh check 5 continues to pass.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/config-check.sh check 5 enforces that every `external: true` volume in docker-compose.yml is matched by a provisioned volume in compose-up.sh's SITE_SUBDIRS list. If the new `opcert` service in docker-compose.yml declares an external volume (a reasonable choice, following the existing pattern for all other services), both SITE_SUBDIRS in compose-up.sh AND this drift gate must still align — but the plan does not enumerate config-check.sh as an affected file or address whether opcert gets an external volume. Failure to extend SITE_SUBDIRS would make check 5 fail CI.
findings.location: What / Compose section; infra/scripts/config-check.sh check 5; infra/scripts/compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an `aws_cloudwatch_log_group` for API Gateway access logs and configure `access_log_settings { destination_arn = ... }` on the `aws_apigatewayv2_stage` `$default` stage, and add an AC assertion that the stage's access log destination is set.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify enabling API Gateway access logging for the new HTTP API. API Gateway access logs are disabled by default and require explicit configuration (`access_log_settings` on the `aws_apigatewayv2_stage`). The new API is the internet-facing SigV4 security boundary; without access logs, authentication failures, unexpected callers, and abuse patterns are invisible.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway HTTP API declaration; AC1 (API Gateway resource assertions)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the plan (or an AC assertion) explicitly acknowledging that `random_password` outputs land in Terraform state and confirming that the S3 backend's `encrypt = true` + existing bucket access controls are accepted as sufficient for this secret class (it is not a primary auth credential), so reviewers and future operators understand the residual exposure is deliberate and bounded.
findings.finding: The `random_password` resource used to generate the X-Opcert-Guard origin-guard value will be stored in plaintext in the Terraform state file. The plan does not acknowledge this or confirm that the S3 state backend's server-side encryption (`encrypt = true`, per `versions.tf`) is sufficient protection for this secret class.
findings.location: ## What — Terraform section: 'Terraform generates the guard value with random_password … stores it as SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard'

findings.finding: AC3's proving command for the materialize-script assertion does not specify how the test verifies the script actually fetches from SSM — whether by mocking the AWS API, running against a live test parameter, or static analysis of the script source. A test that only parses the script statically (e.g. grepping for 'aws ssm get-parameter') does not prove the script works end-to-end (fixture-invalidity defect, taxonomy item 4), while a test requiring live SSM access may not be reproducible in CI without extra setup.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'a test asserts...that the materialize script (per the infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh precedent) renders the guard value from its SSM parameter'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The materialize-script test uses [bats with a mock SSM endpoint / static source assertion + AC5 live coverage rationale] to verify the script reads `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` and renders the value into the nginx config include file.' Pick one approach and state it explicitly so the executor does not have to choose.

findings.finding: AC3 asserts that 'a test asserts... the materialize script renders the guard value from its SSM parameter,' but rendering requires either live SSM access (account-bound, not in-session for a unit test) or a mock/stub strategy. The plan specifies neither; an executor writing a unit test has no guidance on whether to mock SSM, perform a config-content-only inspection, or treat this as an integration test requiring live credentials — leaving a real risk that the assertion is implemented as a weaker script-existence check that does not actually verify rendering behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
